Name: low_latency_ml_inference
Title: Mixed-Precision Quantization for Low-Latency Machine Learning Inference with PAC-Bayes Frameworks and Spectral Stability Bounds
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that by leveraging mixed-precision quantization and PAC-Bayes frameworks, we can develop a low-latency machine learning inference method that maintains high accuracy while reducing computational overhead. The key insight is to use spectral stability bounds to guide the selection of optimal precision levels for different layers of the neural network.
Related Work: Recent works such as SNIP, EMQ, and AMP-ViT have explored mixed-precision quantization for machine learning models. However, these methods focus on training or post-training quantization and do not explicitly consider latency constraints. Our proposal differs from these works by incorporating PAC-Bayes frameworks and spectral stability bounds to optimize precision levels for low-latency inference.
Abstract: This proposal introduces a novel approach to low-latency machine learning inference by leveraging mixed-precision quantization and PAC-Bayes frameworks. We use spectral stability bounds to guide the selection of optimal precision levels for different layers of the neural network, ensuring high accuracy while reducing computational overhead. Our method is designed to be flexible and adaptable to various hardware platforms, making it suitable for a wide range of applications. We evaluate our approach on benchmark datasets and demonstrate its effectiveness in achieving low-latency inference with minimal accuracy loss.
Experiments: Evaluate the proposed method on benchmark datasets (e.g., ImageNet, CIFAR-10) using various neural network architectures (e.g., ResNet, MobileNet)
Compare the performance of our method with state-of-the-art mixed-precision quantization methods (e.g., SNIP, EMQ, AMP-ViT)
Investigate the impact of different precision levels and spectral stability bounds on the accuracy and latency of the proposed method
Conduct ablation studies to analyze the contribution of each component of the proposed method to its overall performance
Risk Factors and Limitations: The proposed method may require significant computational resources to optimize precision levels and spectral stability bounds
The method may not be suitable for very large neural networks or complex datasets due to the increased computational overhead
The choice of spectral stability bounds and precision levels may require careful tuning and expertise in machine learning and signal processing
